relationship_to_restored_text: Interpretive contemplative layer aligned by verse. This does not replace the source-audited restored translation.

verses.mystical_translation: YHWH speaks the closing laws of vows and holy dedication.
A special vow may create a valuation obligation before YHWH.
Adult male valuation is set by the ancient vow schedule.
Adult female valuation is set differently in that ancient economy.
Younger ages receive lower scheduled valuations.
Infants and small children are assigned the smallest vow amounts.
Older adults receive another reduced schedule.
Poverty overrides the table; the priest adjusts to what the hand can reach.
An acceptable offering animal given to YHWH becomes holy.
Manipulative exchange is blocked; both animals become holy.
An animal not used for offering is brought for valuation.
The priest assesses it without pretending all things have the same use.
Redemption adds a fifth.
A house dedicated to YHWH is valued by the priest.
The dedicating owner may redeem it by adding a fifth.
A field dedication is valued by its seed capacity.
A field dedicated at Jubilee receives the full valuation.
Later dedication is reduced by years remaining until Jubilee.
The owner may redeem the field with an added fifth.
A field sold onward after dedication cannot simply be reclaimed again.
At Jubilee the devoted field passes into priestly possession.
Purchased land is distinguished from ancestral possession.
Its holy valuation is calculated only until Jubilee.
Jubilee returns the land to its original holding.
The sanctuary shekel gives a common measure.
Firstborn animals already belong to YHWH and cannot be rededicated.
An unclean firstborn may be redeemed with an added fifth.
What is devoted as irrevocably holy cannot be traded or redeemed.
The severe devoted-person rule is preserved only for deep audit and never for modern harm.
The land's tithe belongs to YHWH.
Redeeming part of the tithe adds a fifth.
Every tenth animal passing under the staff becomes holy.
The tenth is not manipulated by quality-selection or exchange.
Leviticus closes with commands given through Moses at Sinai.

contemplative_note: Leviticus 27 closes the book by putting vows, houses, fields, animals, and tithe under accountable limits. The numbers regulate obligations; they never define a person's sacred worth.
